title: The Nemmeli Project: By Whom, and When?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The translation follows the printed source's chronology, figures, quoted statements and satirical tone. Obvious OCR defects in the derived Tamil reading copy were checked against the source pages; the canonical source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Among the reports published in the newspapers on 23 December 2013 was one under the headline, “New record at Nemmeli desalination plant with 101 per cent drinking-water production.” It stated: “Chief Minister Jayalalithaa recently reviewed Chennai city's water requirements. Following this, the Chennai Metropolitan Water Supply and Sewerage Board took measures to increase drinking-water production. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa inaugurated the Nemmeli seawater desalination plant to meet Chennai's drinking-water needs. Ever since then, the plant has been producing drinking water to meet the city's requirements. It has now achieved a new record in its production capacity by producing 101 per cent drinking water.”

Naturally, we were pleased when we read this report. It says that the Water Board took measures to increase drinking-water production after the Chief Minister conducted a review. Does that mean that, had the Chief Minister not conducted a review, the Board would not have taken measures to increase production?

The Nemmeli drinking-water project was initiated under the DMK government with financial assistance from the Central government. Chief Minister Jayalalithaa inaugurated it at the beginning of this year, on 22 February 2013. At this point, I would like briefly to explain the history of the Nemmeli seawater desalination project.

The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam had continuously urged the Central government to establish a seawater desalination project near Chennai to address the city's drinking-water problem. The United Progressive Alliance government formed at the Centre in 2004 promptly accepted the DMK's request. Consequently, while presenting the 2004-2005 Budget in Parliament, Union Finance Minister P. Chidambaram announced:
“An allocation of Rs. 1,000 crore will be made for a seawater desalination project with a production capacity of 300 million litres per day for Chennai and the cities along the Coromandel Coast.”

Following that announcement, repeated requests were made to the then AIADMK government to obtain the Central assistance and implement the seawater desalination project in Chennai. Nevertheless, the AIADMK government remained negligent until the very end and failed to implement it. In that situation, the DMK pointed this out in the manifesto it issued before the 2006 Tamil Nadu Assembly election and promised:
“Immediate steps will be taken to implement this major drinking-water project once we win the election and form the government.”

In accordance with that promise, after the DMK government was formed in Tamil Nadu in 2006, a project report was prepared to implement a seawater desalination project at Nemmeli in South Chennai. On 27 January 2008, I personally handed that report to the Union Finance Minister and requested immediate approval and financial assistance for the project.

Thereafter, I sent Thambi Stalin, who served as the minister responsible for this department and as Deputy Chief Minister, along with government officials to Delhi to expedite the project. On that basis, the Union Ministry of Urban Development recommended an allocation of Rs. 908.28 crore for it.

The Union government's Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs then approved a grant of Rs. 871.24 crore for the project on 2 January 2009. In March 2009, the Central government released the first instalment of Rs. 300 crore to the Tamil Nadu government. Subsequently, Thambi Stalin, then Deputy Chief Minister, launched the project works on 23 February 2010. During the DMK government's tenure, 60 per cent of the land-development work and 85 per cent of the work to construct the structural supports were completed, while all other works proceeded rapidly and at full pace. Work also advanced quickly on laying 66 kilometres of pipelines to carry the drinking water produced by the project.

A target was set to maintain this pace and complete all project works by December 2011, and the work was accelerated accordingly. The works that should have been completed by December 2011 were delayed through neglect after the AIADMK assumed office, and the inauguration therefore took place only in February 2013.
It is this Nemmeli project that the Water Board now says has achieved 101 per cent production. Reading that report made me happy, as one who had played a major part in bringing the project about.

But at the beginning of this month, another report about the Nemmeli drinking-water project appeared in the newspapers. It stated that “drinking-water production at the Nemmeli plant has been halted for several days in the name of maintenance work, causing a severe drinking-water shortage in several parts of South Chennai.” Reports said that the reverse-osmosis membranes in the plant's machinery had developed a fault; that drinking water had consequently not been properly supplied to Akkarai, Injambakkam, Neelankarai, Vettuvankeni, Palavakkam, Kottivakkam, Thiruvanmiyur and Besant Nagar in South Chennai; and that more than 500,000 people had been affected. At the time, I drew attention to this in the question-and-answer section of *Murasoli*. It was only after I said this that Chief Minister Jayalalithaa held a review meeting on the drinking-water problem.

In recent times, we have repeatedly seen the Chief Minister arrange a review meeting almost every day, seat four particular ministers in the front row, pose as though consulting officials, have a photograph taken and get it published in the newspapers. Those photographs can only make one laugh! Even schoolchildren sit comfortably enough to move their arms and legs a little. But look at the “reverence” with which the ministers sit rigidly in these photographs. Good heavens! The boundless respect these ministers display towards the Chief Minister, bending at the waist, shrinking into themselves and offering pitiful obeisance, makes one understand from the newspaper photographs just how far office can make a human being stoop and cower. Let it be. That is their party affair!

When the Chennai Water Board itself has issued a press release saying that the Nemmeli seawater desalination project, begun under the DMK government, has now stabilised to this extent and set a new record by producing 101 per cent drinking water, would I not be happy as the person who made every initial effort to launch that project?

At this point, another doubt arises. Whenever the Legislative Assembly is in session, the Chief Minister reads a statement each day under Rule 110, does she not? On 16 April 2013, about eight months ago, she read one such statement in the Assembly. It said: “To meet Chennai city's growing drinking-water requirements in keeping with its increasing population, I am pleased to announce that an additional seawater desalination plant with a capacity of 150 million litres per day will be established at a cost of Rs. 1,000 crore on the vacant 10.50 acres adjoining the plant now established at Nemmeli. Once this project is completed, approximately 646,000 residents of Alandur, Perungudi, Kottivakkam, Puzhuthivakkam, Pallikaranai, Madipakkam, Sholinganallur, Karapakkam, Neelankarai, Injambakkam, Semmancheri, Uthandi and Okkiyam Thoraipakkam in the southern part of the expanded Chennai metropolis will benefit.” Chief Minister Jayalalithaa read this and several other project announcements to loud acclaim in the Assembly, and the leaders of the friendly parties she favours all praised it delightedly. Eight months have now passed. What has become of it?

When the Chief Minister summoned Water Board officials and consulted them along with the other ministers, was this new project not discussed? If it was, did the government press release forget to mention that discussion and state what stage the project has reached? Or, because she urgently had to leave for Kodanad on pressing business, was there perhaps no time to discuss it? Who is there to ask questions? They are there only to applaud and fall at her feet!

What on earth is there at Kodanad? The ministers, however, are said to have heaved a great sigh of relief. Do you know why? Apparently, it is the confidence that they will remain ministers at least until “Amma” returns!
Affectionately,
M.K.
26 December 2013
